title: When to self-manage, see a doctor or seek emergency care
summary: Most uncomplicated colds improve with home care, but breathing, hydration, age, risk factors and trajectory determine when to escalate. A sudden deterioration matters more than waiting for an arbitrary day count.
evidenceSnapshot: Emergency care is needed for severe breathing difficulty, blue colour, collapse, confusion or other life-threatening signs. Same-day or early clinical review is appropriate for high-risk people, prolonged fever, dehydration, worsening symptoms or suspected influenza where antivirals may help.

interventions.name: Self-management
interventions.finding: Appropriate when symptoms are mild, breathing and hydration are normal, and the person is gradually stable or improving.
interventions.practical: Rest, fluids, symptom-specific measures and daily reassessment.
interventions.mechanism: Supports recovery while avoiding unnecessary treatment.
interventions.cautions: Use a lower threshold for babies, pregnancy, frailty, immune suppression and chronic heart/lung disease.

interventions.name: GP, pharmacist or telehealth review
interventions.finding: Helps when diagnosis, medicine choice or risk is uncertain.
interventions.practical: Seek review for fever lasting more than about 3 days, symptoms beyond 10 days without improvement, 'double worsening', significant pain or persistent cough.
interventions.mechanism: Clinical assessment identifies complications and targeted treatment.
interventions.cautions: These are prompts, not absolute rules; severe symptoms need earlier care.

interventions.name: Same-day urgent assessment
interventions.finding: Needed for moderate breathing difficulty, dehydration, marked deterioration or high-risk influenza/COVID symptoms.
interventions.practical: Use GP urgent appointments, after-hours services or healthdirect 1800 022 222 in Australia.
interventions.mechanism: Enables examination, oxygen assessment, testing and treatment.
interventions.cautions: Do not drive if faint, confused or severely unwell.

interventions.name: Emergency care / call 000
interventions.finding: Needed for signs of immediate threat to breathing, circulation or consciousness.
interventions.practical: Call 000 for severe breathing difficulty, blue/grey colour, collapse, seizure, severe chest pain, confusion or inability to wake.
interventions.cautions: Do not delay for an online diagnosis.

practicalSteps: Check breathing effort, colour, alertness, fluid intake and urine at least a few times a day.
Record fever pattern, medicines and symptom onset.
Note whether symptoms are steadily improving, static, or improving then worsening again.
Use healthdirect's Symptom Checker or call 1800 022 222 when unsure in Australia.
avoidOrCare: Do not use temperature alone to judge severity.
Do not wait 10 days when breathing, hydration or alertness is abnormal.
Do not take leftover antibiotics; most colds and influenza are viral.
Do not dismiss chest pain, haemoptysis or neurological symptoms as 'just flu'.

myths.claim: Green mucus means antibiotics are needed.
myths.reality: Mucus colour can change during viral illness and does not by itself prove bacterial infection.

myths.claim: If the fever comes down with paracetamol, the illness cannot be serious.
myths.reality: Response to fever medicine does not rule out serious infection.

redFlags: Call 000: severe breathing difficulty, blue/grey lips, collapse, seizure, severe chest pain, confusion or inability to wake
Urgent: baby under 3 months with 38°C or higher
Urgent: signs of dehydration, repeated vomiting, very little urine or inability to drink
Urgent: stiff neck with severe headache, non-blanching rash, coughing blood or rapidly worsening illness
Prompt review: fever over about 3 days, symptoms over 10 days without improvement, or improvement followed by clear worsening
unsettled: No single duration or temperature threshold safely replaces clinical judgement; age, baseline health and symptom pattern change risk.
